title: External Validation of Simplified PTSD Criteria
text: key question developing simplified diagnostic criteria whether combinations optimized one sample generalize different population. PTSDdiag package supports derivation-validation framework : Optimal symptom combinations derived clinical sample (e.g., veterans high PTSD prevalence) combinations validated independent sample (e.g., general population lower prevalence) vignette demonstrates workflow using two simulated datasets included package: simulated_ptsd: clinical veteran sample (N = 5,000, ~94% PTSD prevalence) simulated_ptsd_genpop: general population sample (N = 1,200, ~21% PTSD prevalence) use optimize_combinations() optimize_combinations_clusters() find 10 best six-symptom combinations veteran data, apply general population data using apply_symptom_combinations().

title: External Validation of Simplified PTSD Criteria
code: library("PTSDdiag")

title: External Validation of Simplified PTSD Criteria
text: derivation dataset represents clinical veteran sample high PTSD prevalence. load standardize column names. Let’s check PTSD prevalence sample:
code: # Load the veteran sample data("simulated_ptsd")  # Standardize column names veterans <- rename_ptsd_columns(simulated_ptsd) veterans_total <- calculate_ptsd_total(veterans) veterans_dx <- create_ptsd_diagnosis_nonbinarized(veterans_total) veterans_summary <- summarize_ptsd(veterans_dx) print(veterans_summary) #>   mean_total sd_total n_diagnosed #> 1     57.772 12.36218        4710

title: External Validation of Simplified PTSD Criteria
text: validation dataset represents general population sample realistic, lower PTSD prevalence. Let’s check PTSD prevalence sample: prevalence difference two samples important: diagnostic metrics like PPV NPV strongly affected base rates. simplified criterion works well high-prevalence sample may perform differently individuals PTSD.
code: # Load the general population sample data("simulated_ptsd_genpop")  # Standardize column names genpop <- rename_ptsd_columns(simulated_ptsd_genpop) genpop_total <- calculate_ptsd_total(genpop) genpop_dx <- create_ptsd_diagnosis_nonbinarized(genpop_total) genpop_summary <- summarize_ptsd(genpop_dx) print(genpop_summary) #>   mean_total sd_total n_diagnosed #> 1   23.63667 15.47307         252

code: results_nonhier <- optimize_combinations(   veterans,   n_symptoms = 6,   n_required = 4,   n_top = 10,   score_by = "false_cases" )  # View the 10 best symptom combinations results_nonhier$best_symptoms  # View diagnostic metrics on the derivation sample results_nonhier$summary

title: External Validation of Simplified PTSD Criteria
text: hierarchical approach ensures combination includes least one symptom DSM-5 criterion cluster, maintaining diagnostic structure.
code: # Define the PCL-5 cluster structure pcl5_clusters <- list(   B = 1:5,     # Intrusion   C = 6:7,     # Avoidance   D = 8:14,    # Negative alterations in cognitions and mood   E = 15:20    # Alterations in arousal and reactivity )  results_hier <- optimize_combinations_clusters(   veterans,   n_symptoms = 6,   n_required = 4,   n_top = 10,   score_by = "false_cases",   clusters = pcl5_clusters )  # View the 10 best symptom combinations results_hier$best_symptoms  # View diagnostic metrics on the derivation sample results_hier$summary

title: External Validation of Simplified PTSD Criteria
text: comparing derivation validation performance, several points important consider: Sensitivity may decrease validation sample simplified criteria overfit derivation sample’s specific symptom patterns. Specificity often changes substantially high-prevalence low-prevalence settings pool true negatives much larger general population. PPV (Positive Predictive Value) strongly influenced base rates. low-prevalence setting (~20%), even highly specific test false positives relative true positives. typically results lower PPV compared derivation sample. NPV (Negative Predictive Value) tends high low-prevalence settings negative results true negatives. Hierarchical vs. Non-Hierarchical: hierarchical approach may show higher specificity general population requiring cluster representation acts additional filter, reducing false positives. non-hierarchical approach may show higher sensitivity less restrictive.

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: PTSDdiag package provides tools analyzing optimizing PTSD diagnostic criteria using PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist DSM-5) data. Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) diagnosis traditionally requires complex evaluation multiple symptom criteria across different clusters. package aims simplify process maintaining diagnostic accuracy. vignette demonstrates use package : Import prepare PCL-5 data Calculate basic descriptive statistics reliability metrics Find optimal six-symptom combinations PTSD diagnosis Compare different diagnostic approaches

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: can install released version CRAN: install development version GitHub:
code: install.packages("PTSDdiag") # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("TobiasRSpiller/PTSDdiag")

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: PTSDdiag installed, can loaded usual way. Load additional packages:
code: library("PTSDdiag") library(psych)     # For reliability analysis

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: package includes simulated dataset mirrors hypothetical PCL-5-assessments. contains 5,000 simulated patient responses, rating 20 PTSD symptoms according DSM-5 criteria. Rating Scale symptom rated 5-point scale: 0 = 1 = little bit 2 = Moderately 3 = Quite bit 4 = Extremely Symptom Clusters PCL-5 organizes symptoms four distinct clusters according DSM-5 criteria: Symptoms 1-5: Criterion B (Intrusion) Symptoms 6-7: Criterion C (Avoidance) Symptoms 8-14: Criterion D (Negative alterations cognitions mood) Symptoms 15-20: Criterion E (Alterations arousal reactivity) Data Format Requirements Input data must : Numeric values 0-4 missing values 20 columns (one per symptom) Row-wise observations Let’s load included sample data: take look first rows sample data:

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: first step standardize column names consistent analysis. standardization, columns might various names: standardization, columns named systematically:
code: #  Example of potential input formats names(simulated_ptsd) #>  [1] "S1"  "S2"  "S3"  "S4"  "S5"  "S6"  "S7"  "S8"  "S9"  "S10" "S11" "S12" #> [13] "S13" "S14" "S15" "S16" "S17" "S18" "S19" "S20" # Rename columns to standard format (symptom_1 through symptom_20) simulated_ptsd_renamed <- rename_ptsd_columns(simulated_ptsd)  # Show new names names(simulated_ptsd_renamed) #>  [1] "symptom_1"  "symptom_2"  "symptom_3"  "symptom_4"  "symptom_5"  #>  [6] "symptom_6"  "symptom_7"  "symptom_8"  "symptom_9"  "symptom_10" #> [11] "symptom_11" "symptom_12" "symptom_13" "symptom_14" "symptom_15" #> [16] "symptom_16" "symptom_17" "symptom_18" "symptom_19" "symptom_20"

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: ’ll now process data several steps calculate scores determine diagnoses: summary statistics provide: Mean total score: Indicates average symptom severity sample Standard deviation: Shows spread severity scores Number diagnosed cases: Based full DSM-5 criteria
code: # Step 1: Calculate total scores (range 0-80) simulated_ptsd_total <- calculate_ptsd_total(simulated_ptsd_renamed)  # Step 2: Apply DSM-5 diagnostic criteria and determine PTSD diagnoses simulated_ptsd_total_diagnosed <- create_ptsd_diagnosis_nonbinarized(simulated_ptsd_total)  # Step 3: Generate summary statistics summary_stats <- summarize_ptsd(simulated_ptsd_total_diagnosed) print(summary_stats) #>   mean_total sd_total n_diagnosed #> 1     57.772 12.36218        4710

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: Now come actual analysis. Current PTSD diagnosis requires evaluating 20 symptoms across four clusters complex rules. goal identify simplified diagnostic criteria : Reduce number symptoms evaluate (20 6) Preserve polythetic nature diagnosis allow representation cluster, least four evaluated six symptoms must present diagnosis. Maintain diagnostic accuracy like identify three six-symptom combinations best represent group PTSD patients compared original DSM-5 criteria. determine optimal six-symptom combinations two different structural approaches (hierarchical approach, requiring least one symptom cluster, non-hierarchical approach, ignoring cluster membership).

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: First, let’s find three optimal six-symptom combinations, least four symptoms must present diagnosis, one symptom DSM-5 criterion cluster must included. approach maintains hierarchical structure PTSD diagnosis. reminder, symptom clusters PCL-5: Items 1-5: Intrusion symptoms (Criterion B) Items 6-7: Avoidance symptoms (Criterion C) Items 8-14: Negative alterations cognitions mood (Criterion D) Items 15-20: Alterations arousal reactivity (Criterion E) definition “optimal combination” can determined score_by argument. Optimization can based either: Minimizing false cases (false positives false negatives) Minimizing false negatives (newly non-diagnosed cases) example, want miss diagnoses possible compared original DSM-5 criteria, want minimize false negative cases (newly_nondiagnosed). Understanding Output function returns three key elements. Let’s take look . Selected Symptom Combinations: Data comparing original DSM-5 diagnosis diagnoses based three best combinations Summary table showing diagnostic accuracy metrics combination summary table includes: Total Diagnosed: Number percentage cases diagnosed Total Non-Diagnosed: Number percentage non-diagnosed cases True Positive/Negative: Agreement original criteria False Positive/Negative: Disagreement original criteria Sensitivity/Specificity: Diagnostic accuracy measures PPV/NPV: Predictive values
code: # Find best combinations with hierarchical approach, minimizing false negatives best_combinations_hierarchical <- analyze_best_six_symptoms_four_required_clusters(   simulated_ptsd_renamed,   score_by = "newly_nondiagnosed" ) best_combinations_hierarchical$best_symptoms # Shows true/false values for original vs. new criteria head(best_combinations_hierarchical$diagnosis_comparison, 10) best_combinations_hierarchical$summary

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: identifying optimal symptom combinations, ’s crucial validate performance independent data. PTSDdiag package provides two validation approaches: Holdout Validation Cross-Validation.

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: Holdout Validation splits data training test sets, allowing us evaluate well identified symptom combinations generalize new data. Results Holdout Validation results show symptom combinations perform data weren’t trained , providing realistic estimate diagnostic accuracy. Examining Results Non-Hierarchical Model: Examining Results Hierarchical Model:
code: # Perform holdout validation with 70/30 split validation_results <- holdout_validation(   simulated_ptsd_renamed,   train_ratio = 0.7,   score_by = "newly_nondiagnosed",   seed = 123 ) # Best combinations identified on training data validation_results$without_clusters$best_combinations  # Performance summary on test data validation_results$without_clusters$summary # Best combinations identified on training data (with cluster representation) validation_results$with_clusters$best_combinations  # Performance summary on test data validation_results$with_clusters$summary

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: robust assessment, k-fold Cross-Validation tests stability symptom combinations across multiple data splits. Results Fold function provides detailed results fold. Examining Results Non-Hierarchical Model: Examining Results Hierarchical Model: Stable Combinations Across Folds certain symptom combinations appear multiple folds, function calculates average performance. Examining Results Non-Hierarchical Model: Examining Results Hierarchical Model: Interpreting Cross-Validation Results Cross-Validation helps identify: Consistency: Whether symptom combinations selected across different data subsets Stability: diagnostic accuracy varies across folds Generalization: Average performance metrics provide robust estimates real-world performance Key metrics examine: Splits_Appeared: many folds selected combination (higher = stable) Sensitivity: Proportion true PTSD cases correctly identified Specificity: Proportion non-PTSD cases correctly identified PPV/NPV: Predictive values indicating diagnostic confidence
code: # Perform 5-fold cross-validation cv_results <- cross_validation(   simulated_ptsd_renamed,   k = 5,   score_by = "newly_nondiagnosed",   seed = 123 ) # Summary statistics for each fold (non-hierarchical model) cv_results$without_clusters$summary_by_fold # Summary statistics for each fold (hierarchical model) cv_results$with_clusters$summary_by_fold # Check for combinations that appeared in multiple folds (non-hierarchical) if (!is.null(cv_results$without_clusters$combinations_summary)) {   print("Stable combinations in non-hierarchical model:")   cv_results$without_clusters$combinations_summary } else {   print("No combinations appeared in multiple folds for the non-hierarchical model") } # Check for combinations that appeared in multiple folds (hierarchical) if (!is.null(cv_results$with_clusters$combinations_summary)) {   print("Stable combinations in hierarchical model:")   cv_results$with_clusters$combinations_summary } else {   print("No combinations appeared in multiple folds for the hierarchical model") }

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: validation methods serve complementary purposes. Holdout Validation: Simpler faster Single train-test split Good initial assessment May less stable small datasets Cross-Validation: computationally intensive Multiple train-test splits Better estimate generalization Identifies stable symptom patterns
code: # Example: Compare sensitivity from both methods # Note: Results will vary based on random splits  # Holdout Validation sensitivity (first combination, non-hierarchical) holdout_summary <- validation_results$without_clusters$summary$x$data if (nrow(holdout_summary) > 1) {   holdout_sensitivity <- holdout_summary[2, "Sensitivity"]   print(paste("Holdout sensitivity for first combination:",                round(holdout_sensitivity, 3))) }  # Cross-Validation average sensitivity (if stable combinations exist) if (!is.null(cv_results$without_clusters$combinations_summary)) {   cv_summary <- cv_results$without_clusters$combinations_summary$x$data   if (nrow(cv_summary) > 0) {     cv_sensitivity <- cv_summary[1, "Sensitivity"]     print(paste("Cross-validation average sensitivity:",                  round(cv_sensitivity, 3)))   } }

code: # Create sample diagnostic data set.seed(123) n_cases <- 100 sample_data <- data.frame(   PTSD_orig = sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), n_cases, replace = TRUE),   PTSD_alt1 = sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), n_cases, replace = TRUE),   PTSD_alt2 = sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), n_cases, replace = TRUE) )  # Calculate diagnostic metrics diagnostic_metrics <- summarize_ptsd_changes(sample_data) diagnostic_metrics #>              column diagnosed non_diagnosed newly_diagnosed newly_nondiagnosed #> PTSD_orig PTSD_orig        57            43               0                  0 #> PTSD_alt1 PTSD_alt1        46            54              20                 31 #> PTSD_alt2 PTSD_alt2        51            49              26                 32 #>           true_positive true_negative true_cases false_cases sensitivity #> PTSD_orig            57            43        100           0   1.0000000 #> PTSD_alt1            26            23         49          51   0.4561404 #> PTSD_alt2            25            17         42          58   0.4385965 #>           specificity       ppv       npv diagnosed_percent #> PTSD_orig   1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000                57 #> PTSD_alt1   0.5348837 0.5652174 0.4259259                46 #> PTSD_alt2   0.3953488 0.4901961 0.3469388                51 #>           non_diagnosed_percent #> PTSD_orig                    43 #> PTSD_alt1                    54 #> PTSD_alt2                    49

title: Write symptom combinations to a JSON file — write_combinations
text: combinations list integer vectors specifying symptom combinations, full result object optimize_combinations / optimize_combinations_clusters (contains $best_symptoms). file Character string. Path output JSON file. n_required Integer specifying many symptoms must present positive diagnosis (default: 4). value stored file read_combinations can retrieve . clusters NULL (default) non-hierarchical combinations, named list integer vectors defining cluster structure hierarchical combinations. Stored file use apply_symptom_combinations. n_symptoms Integer NULL. Number symptoms per combination. NULL (default), inferred length first combination. score_by Character NULL. scoring criterion used optimization ("false_cases" "newly_nondiagnosed"). Stored metadata reproducibility. NULL (default), omitted file. description Character string. Optional free-text description derivation context (e.g., sample characteristics, dataset name). Default empty string.

title: PTSDdiag 0.2.0
text: New generalized optimization functions optimize_combinations() optimize_combinations_clusters() allow configurable number symptoms per combination (n_symptoms), required threshold (n_required), number top results (n_top), custom cluster definitions (clusters). New apply_symptom_combinations() function external validation: apply pre-derived combinations new dataset compare DSM-5 baseline. New write_combinations() read_combinations() functions exporting importing optimized symptom combinations human-readable JSON files, enabling collaboration across research groups without sharing raw data. Original functions analyze_best_six_symptoms_four_required() analyze_best_six_symptoms_four_required_clusters() preserved backward-compatible wrappers PCL-5 defaults. holdout_validation() cross_validation() now accept n_symptoms, n_required, n_top parameters. Internal refactoring: shared validation diagnosis logic consolidated reusable internal helpers.

title: PTSDdiag 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-02-13 Initial CRAN submission. Core analysis functions identifying optimal 6-symptom PTSD diagnostic combinations using PCL-5 data. Hierarchical (cluster-based) non-hierarchical analysis approaches. DSM-5 diagnostic criteria implementation (binarized non-binarized). Diagnostic accuracy metrics: sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV. Holdout k-fold cross-validation methods model evaluation. Simulated PCL-5 dataset 5,000 observations demonstration.
